title: AIC Gets Flashy with 32 SSD Bay JBOF Server for Key Value Caching
gist: AIC's F2032-01-G6 is a PCIe Gen6, 32-bay E3 JBOF storage server built around NVIDIA BlueField-4 DPUs and Broadcom's PEX90144 switch, positioned as KV-cache infrastructure for NVIDIA's Context Memory Storage platform feeding Vera Rubin racks, illustrating how fast local storage is becoming a mandatory adjunct to GPU inference clusters.

insights.claim: AIC's F2032-01-G6 is a 2U JBOF (just-a-bunch-of-flash) appliance holding 32 PCIe Gen6 E3-format SSDs, capable of storing up to 8PB when populated with 256TB drives.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Shows how much raw flash density vendors are now packing per rack unit to keep pace with AI inference storage/caching demand.
insights.quote: When fully equipped with 256TB SSDs, a single F2032-G6 can store up to 8PB of data.

insights.claim: The F2032-01-G6 succeeds AIC's PCIe Gen5-era F2026-01-G5, which used 26 2.5-inch U.2 bays; the new model is a full-platform upgrade in storage format, I/O, and networking rather than an incremental refresh.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Marks the generational jump from PCIe Gen5/U.2 to Gen6/E3 that storage vendors are making in lockstep with new GPU platform cycles.
insights.locator: The F2032-01-G6 is a successor of sorts

insights.claim: The server supports both long (E3.L) and short (E3.S) 7.5mm E3-format SSDs in hot-swappable trays, with thin vents between drive pairs for airflow.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: E3 form-factor flexibility lets AIC serve both max-capacity and max-performance SKUs from one chassis design.
insights.locator: AIC has designed this latest JBOF appliance to use E3-form-factor SSDs

insights.claim: QLC NAND lets the F2032-01-G6 hit its 8PB ceiling in 2U, while TLC NAND caps capacity at roughly half that amount, forcing a density-versus-endurance/performance tradeoff for data center buyers.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Buyers choosing this class of JBOF must trade capacity for endurance, a key spec differentiator when sizing KV-cache tiers.
insights.quote: For data centers happy with QLC memory, this puts the current limit on the box at 8PB in 2U, while TLC NAND can be had at around half that capacity.
insights.locator: With E3.L drives available in capacities up to 256TB each

insights.claim: The internal I/O fabric is built around Broadcom's PEX90144, a 144-lane PCIe Gen6 switch also seen inside the Dell PowerEdge XE7745 chassis.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Broadcom's PCIe switch silicon is becoming a common backbone component across multiple vendors' Gen6 storage platforms, a chokepoint/beneficiary to watch in the supply chain.
insights.locator: the system's internal I/O fabric is built around Broadcom's massive 144-lane PEX90144

insights.claim: AIC likely routes only two PCIe lanes to each SSD (based on the prior-generation design), and the move to PCIe Gen6 is intended to offset the bandwidth deficit that such a x2 lane configuration would otherwise create.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Explains the engineering rationale for jumping to Gen6: it's less about raw per-drive speed and more about preserving bandwidth while maximizing drive count per chassis.
insights.locator: AIC has not published a detailed architectural diagram

insights.claim: The JBOF is driven by a pair of independent nodes, each with its own power supply and capable of housing up to two network expansion devices or even a GPU.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Modular node design signals AIC is building flexibility for customers to add compute/GPU capability directly into the storage appliance, blurring the line between storage and compute nodes.
insights.locator: the system is driven by a pair of nodes

insights.claim: The F2032-01-G6 is designed to be controlled by NVIDIA BlueField-4 DPUs (Grace CPU plus ConnectX-9 NIC), which provide 800Gb Ethernet or InfiniBand networking and PCIe Gen6 I/O, acting as storage controllers gatewaying all SSD access.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Deep dependency on NVIDIA's DPU roadmap ties AIC's product cadence and margin structure directly to NVIDIA's BlueField release schedule.
insights.locator: The JBOF appliance is designed to be controlled by at least one of NVIDIA's BlueField-4 DPUs

insights.claim: The F2032-01-G6 brings AIC into closer alignment with NVIDIA's Context Memory Storage (CMX) platform standard, which is designed for integration into Vera Rubin racks.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA is standardizing an ecosystem-wide KV-cache storage spec (CMX) that storage OEMs must design toward to stay compatible with future Vera Rubin deployments.
insights.locator: The latest JBOF box from AIC also brings the company closer in alignment

insights.claim: The chief intended use case for the F2032-01-G6 is to serve as a key-value cache within NVIDIA's Context Memory Storage platform, NVIDIA's own KV-caching solution backing Vera Rubin systems.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Confirms KV-cache appliances are being purpose-built as first-class citizens of next-gen GPU rack architectures rather than generic storage add-ons.
insights.locator: the chief use for the JBOF appliance is to serve as a KV cache

insights.claim: AIC positions the F2032-01-G6 as a high-availability system: supporting DPUs in pairs lets a second node take over fully if one DPU (or part of one) fails, keeping the JBOF appliance online.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Redundant DPU-per-node design targets enterprise/hyperscale uptime requirements for mission-critical KV-cache tiers sitting in the inference hot path.
insights.locator: AIC is specifically positioning the F2032-01-G6 as a high-availability system

themes.theme: Market implications for AI infrastructure capex

insights.claim: Sizable KV caches are quickly becoming a preferred, if not mandatory, component for large inference clusters to improve performance, and JBOF servers like the F2032-01-G6 are expected to become increasingly common in AI data centers.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Points to a growing, distinct capex line item (dedicated flash KV-cache appliances) within AI infrastructure buildouts, beyond GPUs and primary storage.
insights.quote: With sizable KV caches quickly becoming a preferred (if not mandatory) component for large inference clusters to bolster their performance, JBOF servers such as AIC's F2032-01-G6 are slated to become an increasingly common sight within AI data centers.

insights.claim: Storing key values physically close to the GPUs performing inference is increasingly critical to scaling inference performance, driving demand to place SSD storage as near to GPU servers as possible.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Explains the physical/architectural driver behind a new category of near-GPU flash appliances, relevant for evaluating datacenter interconnect and rack topology investments.
insights.quote: Storing key values close to the GPUs performing inference is increasingly critical to scaling performance, which means placing SSDs as close to those servers as is reasonably possible

insights.claim: AIC is well positioned to capture share of the emerging KV-cache JBOF market by leveraging its prior experience with PCIe Gen5 and BlueField-3-era hardware to move quickly into the BlueField-4/Vera Rubin generation.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: Frames AIC as an incumbent-advantaged supplier in the nascent NVIDIA-aligned KV-cache storage niche, useful for tracking which OEMs benefit from Vera Rubin-cycle spend.
insights.quote: AIC, in turn, is well positioned to capture a chunk of this market, leveraging its experience with PCIe Gen5 and BlueField-3-era hardware to hit the ground running with the BlueField-4/Vera Rubin generation.

insights.claim: Beyond KV caching, AIC is also pitching the F2032-01-G6's flexible configuration for general use cases such as object storage, scale-out file systems, and flash storage tiering.
insights.speaker: Ryan Smith
insights.so_what: A multi-use-case pitch diversifies AIC's addressable market beyond the still-nascent NVIDIA CMX/KV-cache use case, hedging against slower-than-expected Vera Rubin adoption.
